instructions: There are 10 questions in total, consisting of 8 one-mark and 2 two-mark questions.
A wrong answer will incur a penalty of 0.33 marks for each one-mark question and 0.66 marks for each two-mark question.

q.value: A periodic signal is given by $x(t)=4\cos(\omega_0t)+3\sin(2\omega_0t)+2\cos(5\omega_0t)+6\cos(7\omega_0t).$ The ratio of the power contained in the odd harmonics to that contained in the even harmonics is:   1 Mark

explanation.value: Power contributed by a sinusoidal component of amplitude $A$ is $A^2/2.$ Odd harmonics are the 1st, 5th and 7th harmonics. Hence $P_{\text{odd}}=\frac{4^2}{2}+\frac{2^2}{2}+\frac{6^2}{2}=8+2+18=28.$ The only even harmonic is the 2nd harmonic, so $P_{\text{even}}=\frac{3^2}{2}=\frac{9}{2}.$ Therefore $\dfrac{P_{\text{odd}}}{P_{\text{even}}}=\dfrac{28}{9/2}=\dfrac{56}{9}.$

q.value: A real periodic signal satisfies $x(t)=x(-t)$ and $x\left(t+\frac{T}{2}\right)=-x(t).$ Which Fourier series representation is correct?   1 Mark

options: Only cosine terms of odd harmonics are present.
Only sine terms of odd harmonics are present.
Both sine and cosine terms of odd harmonics are present.
Only the DC component is present.

answer.value: Only cosine terms of odd harmonics are present.

explanation.value: Even symmetry eliminates all sine coefficients ($b_n=0$). Half-wave symmetry eliminates the DC component and all even harmonics. Hence only odd cosine harmonics remain.

q.value:
The complex Fourier series coefficients of a periodic signal are $C_{\pm1}=1,$ $C_{\pm2}=\frac12,$ $C_{\pm3}=\frac13,$ and all remaining coefficients are zero. The average power of the signal is:
1 Mark
If $x(t)\longleftrightarrow C_k$ in the complex Fourier series, then the Fourier coefficients of $y(t)=x\left(t-\frac{T}{8}\right)$ are:   1 Mark

explanation.value: For a time delay $t_0,$ the coefficients become $C'_k=C_ke^{-jk\omega_0t_0}.$ Here $t_0=T/8$ and $\omega_0=2\pi/T,$ hence $\omega_0t_0=\pi/4.$ Therefore $C'_k=C_ke^{-jk\pi/4}.$

q.value: A periodic signal has Fourier series coefficients $a_0=4,$ $a_1=6,$ $b_1=8,$ and all remaining coefficients are zero. The RMS value of the signal is:   1 Mark

explanation.value: Using Parseval's theorem, the average power of the signal is $$P=a_0^2+\frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(a_n^2+b_n^2).$$ Since only $a_1$ and $b_1$ are non-zero, $$P=4^2+\frac{1}{2}(6^2+8^2)$$=$$16+\frac{1}{2}(36+64)=16+50=66.$$ Therefore, the RMS value is $$x_{\rm rms}=\sqrt{P}=\sqrt{66}.$$

q.value:
A periodic signal $x(t)$ has complex Fourier series coefficients $C_k$. If $y(t)=\dfrac{d}{dt}x(t)$, then the complex Fourier series coefficient of $y(t)$ corresponding to the $k^{\rm th}$ harmonic is:
  1 Mark

explanation.value: The complex Fourier series of $x(t)$ is $$x(t)=\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty}C_k e^{jk\omega_0 t}.$$ Differentiating both sides, $$y(t)=\frac{d}{dt}x(t)=\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty}jk\omega_0 C_k e^{jk\omega_0 t}.$$ Hence, the complex Fourier series coefficient of $y(t)$ is $$C_k^{(y)}=jk\omega_0 C_k.$$

q.value: A periodic signal $x(t)$ with fundamental frequency $\omega_0$ has complex Fourier series coefficients $C_k$. A new signal is defined as $$y(t)=x\left(t-\frac{T_0}{4}\right),$$ where $T_0=\frac{2\pi}{\omega_0}$. If $C_2=3-j$ and $C_{-2}=3+j$, then the coefficient $D_2$ of the complex Fourier series of $y(t)$ is:  1 Mark

explanation.value: For a time shift, if $$y(t)=x(t-t_0),$$ then the complex Fourier series coefficients are $$D_k=C_k e^{-jk\omega_0 t_0}.$$ Here, $$t_0=\frac{T_0}{4}=\frac{\pi}{2\omega_0}.$$ Therefore, $$D_2=(3-j)e^{-j2\omega_0\left(\frac{\pi}{2\omega_0}\right)}$$=$$(3-j)e^{-j\pi}=-(3-j)=-3+j.$$ Hence, the correct answer is $$\boxed{-3+j}.$$

q.value:
A periodic signal $x(t)$ has complex Fourier series coefficients $C_k$. A new signal is defined as $$y(t)=x(-t).$$ If $C_2=4-j$ and $C_{-2}=4+j$, then the complex Fourier series coefficient $D_2$ of $y(t)$ is:
  1 Mark

explanation.value: For time reversal, if $$y(t)=x(-t),$$ then the complex Fourier series coefficients satisfy $$D_k=C_{-k}.$$ Hence, $$D_2=C_{-2}=4+j.$$ Therefore, the correct answer is $$\boxed{4+j}.$$

q.value: A periodic signal $x(t)$ has complex Fourier series coefficients $C_k$. A new signal is defined as $$y(t)=x(t)e^{j3\omega_0 t}.$$ If $$C_{-1}=2+j,\; C_0=4$$,$$\; C_1=3-j,\; C_2=1+2j,$$ and all other coefficients are zero, then the complex Fourier series coefficient $D_2$ of $y(t)$ is:   2 Mark

explanation.value: The frequency shift property of the complex Fourier series states that if $$y(t)=x(t)e^{jm\omega_0 t},$$ then the Fourier series coefficients satisfy $$D_k=C_{k-m}.$$ Here, $m=3$. Therefore, $$D_2=C_{2-3}=C_{-1}=2+j.$$ Hence, the correct answer is $$\boxed{2+j}.$$

q.value: A real-valued periodic signal $x(t)$ has complex Fourier series coefficients satisfying $C_1=2-j$ and $C_2=1+j$. A new signal is defined as $$y(t)=x\left(t-\frac{T_0}{4}\right)e^{-j\omega_0 t},$$ where $T_0=\frac{2\pi}{\omega_0}$. The complex Fourier series coefficient $D_0$ of $y(t)$ is:   2 Mark

explanation.value: First apply the time-shift property. If $$z(t)=x(t-t_0),$$ then $$Z_k=C_ke^{-jk\omega_0t_0}.$$ Here, $$t_0=\frac{T_0}{4},$$ so $$Z_k=C_ke^{-jk\pi/2}.$$ Next, multiplying by $$e^{-j\omega_0t}$$ shifts the coefficients by one index: $$D_k=Z_{k+1}.$$ Hence, $$D_0=Z_1=C_1e^{-j\pi/2}$$=$$(2-j)(-j)=-1-2j.$$ Therefore, the correct answer is $$\boxed{-1-2j}.$$
